title: As the Tide Turns Against Putin, Beware the Drowning Man
gist: Oxford historian Peter Frankopan argues Russia's war in Ukraine is now costing it enormous casualties, refining capacity, and economic stability — with deep Ukrainian drone strikes cutting Russian refining capacity by 700,000 bpd, fuel rationing spreading across half of Russia's regions, and domestic elite discontent rising — and warns that as Putin's options narrow he is likely to respond not with negotiation but with escalating threats and hybrid attacks against Ukraine and the West, a 'drowning man syndrome' risk compounded by Russia's lowered nuclear threshold.

insights.claim: Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine has now lasted longer than both the Soviet fight against Nazi Germany and all of World War I, with Russian forces having ceased making significant battlefield gains and some data indicating they lost territory in April and May 2026.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A concrete duration/momentum benchmark showing the war has structurally stalled for Russia, undercutting any near-term negotiated-victory narrative.
insights.locator: The 'special military operation' he launched

insights.claim: GCHQ director Anna Keast-Butler said new intelligence indicates Russian war deaths have likely reached almost half a million, with various Western sources putting total Russian casualties at significantly more than 1 million; Russia is now suffering roughly eight killed or seriously wounded for every one Ukrainian casualty, with monthly losses exceeding 30,000 in 2026.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Quantifies an attrition ratio (8:1) that is financially and demographically unsustainable at current recruitment rates, a leading indicator for eventual force degradation.
insights.locator: Last month, Anna Keast-Butler

insights.claim: Russia is offering enlistment bonuses as high as $80,000 and up to $140,000 in debt relief to attract recruits, yet according to Russian military bloggers new recruits' average life expectancy from training to death is 10 days to three weeks, and combat survival time once deployed averages just 20-35 minutes — a shift driven largely by drones becoming the primary killing platform.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: The extreme financial incentives needed to sustain recruitment, paired with near-immediate battlefield attrition, is a direct signal of how deeply drone warfare has degraded Russian force viability.
insights.locator: Those who do have little to look forward to

themes.theme: The deep-strike campaign against Russia's energy infrastructure

insights.claim: Ukraine's domestically produced long-range drones (FP-1, FP-2, Hornet) now regularly strike deep inside Russia; a mid-June attack on Moscow reportedly disabled the Russian capital's largest oil refinery until 2027, and Reuters estimates Ukrainian drones have cut Russian refining capacity by 700,000 barrels per day.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A 700,000 bpd refining-capacity cut is a materially significant fraction of Russian output — a leading indicator for tighter domestic fuel supply and reduced export capacity through 2027.
insights.locator: Kyiv's new, domestically produced long-range drones

insights.claim: Ukrainian drones struck an oil terminal in St. Petersburg on the opening morning of the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum in June, and hit the Antipinsky oil refinery in Tyumen, western Siberia — more than 1,000 miles from the front and converting about 160,000 barrels of oil to fuel per day — twice within days, prompting facilities as far as Vladivostok and Sakhalin to invest in anti-drone defenses.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Demonstrates Ukraine's strike range now covers the entirety of Russian territory, forcing costly defensive capex at facilities previously considered safe by distance alone.
insights.locator: On the first morning of the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum

insights.claim: Russia's diesel output fell a further 10% in May, Moscow temporarily banned gasoline exports, more than half of Russia's regions have started rationing fuel, and Crimean authorities have suspended gasoline sales to the public.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Widespread regional fuel rationing is a hard, observable proxy for the drone campaign's macroeconomic bite, useful for tracking Russian energy-sector stress in near-real time.
insights.locator: Russians are feeling increasing economic pain

themes.theme: A war-strained economy nearing breaking point

insights.claim: Germany's Federal Intelligence Service (BND) reported that Russian military spending already accounts for half of Russia's entire state budget, significantly more than the Kremlin has publicly acknowledged.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A 50%-of-budget military spend implies limited fiscal room to both sustain the war and avoid domestic austerity — a structural vulnerability for any war-financing analysis.
insights.locator: Earlier this year, Germany's Federal Intelligence Service

insights.claim: Thomas Nilsson, head of Sweden's Military Intelligence and Security Service, assessed that the Russian economy 'can only enter one of two scenarios: long-term decline or shock,' either of which leads to 'financial disaster.'
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
entities: Sweden Military Intelligence and Security Service
insights.so_what: A binary downside framework from a state intelligence source — useful shorthand for scenario-planning Russian macro risk (gradual erosion vs. sudden shock).
insights.quote: can only enter one of two scenarios: long-term decline or shock
insights.locator: Thomas Nilsson, head of Sweden's Military Intelligence

insights.claim: Russian Central Bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina warned of high inflation and 'almost permanent' economic deterioration and said Russia faces a labor shortage for the first time in modern history; rumors have since swirled that she may be replaced because her warnings are unacceptable to Putin.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A sitting central bank governor publicly flagging structural deterioration — and facing possible ouster for it — signals real internal policy conflict over how much economic pain to disclose.
insights.locator: In April, Russian Central Bank Governor Elvira Nabiullina

insights.claim: Russian residential real estate construction fell almost 40% in Q1 2026 versus the same period in 2025, commercial warehouse demand fell by a similar margin, and the number of canceled civilian passenger flights has quadrupled since the start of the year.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Multiple independent sectoral indicators (construction, logistics demand, aviation) corroborating the same deceleration story strengthens confidence this is a broad-based economic slowdown, not a single-sector anomaly.
insights.locator: Other parts of the economy do not look promising

insights.claim: To fill state coffers, Russian oligarchs have made 'voluntary contributions' expected to bring in about $4 billion by the end of 2026, and the Kremlin confiscated the assets of Rusagro founder Vadim Moshkovich, worth over $7 billion.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Asset seizures from a formerly protected oligarch signal the regime is now willing to alienate its own elite base for war funding — a watch-item for elite-cohesion risk around Putin.
insights.locator: To fill state coffers, oligarchs have made

insights.claim: An anonymous senior Russian businessman told the Financial Times that elites are 'in full agreement this is a catastrophe,' while a Guardian source described a growing realization that 'utterly senseless, self-destructive decisions keep being made' and that 'any sense of a future has disappeared,' though no one expects sudden collapse.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: First-hand elite sentiment shifting from patriotic support to open despair is an early qualitative indicator of eroding regime legitimacy, though the source cautions against expecting imminent collapse.
insights.quote: there is a growing realisation that utterly senseless, self-destructive decisions keep being made
insights.locator: The Guardian quoted another anonymous business leader

insights.claim: Russian blogger Maxim Kalashnikov wrote 'We are on the verge of dramatic events,' explicitly asking whether Russia is 'heading towards something like 1917' — the year the Russian Empire and its military collapsed.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A fringe but notable domestic commentary point invoking regime-collapse historical parallels — worth tracking as a sentiment indicator even if the author judges outright revolution unlikely.
insights.quote: We are on the verge of dramatic events
insights.locator: angry Russian bloggers think otherwise

insights.claim: Frankopan argues the more likely outcome is not revolution but that accumulating economic pain and war disillusionment convince factions within Moscow's elite that a change in leadership or direction is needed, since 'today's cracks can become tomorrow's fissures.'
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Frames the realistic base case for regime change risk as elite-driven rather than street revolution — relevant for scenario planning on Russian political-risk timelines.
insights.quote: today's cracks can become tomorrow's fissures
insights.locator: Rather than another revolution

insights.claim: Frankopan's central thesis is that as conditions worsen on the battlefield, in the economy, and personally for Putin, there is a rising risk of 'drowning man syndrome' — a struggling swimmer pushing others under the water in a desperate attempt to stay afloat — meaning Putin is likely to respond to weakness with escalation rather than compromise.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: The article's core actionable framework: rising Russian weakness should be read as an escalation-risk signal, not a de-escalation one, for anyone pricing tail risk in Ukraine/NATO-adjacent assets.
insights.locator: there is the risk that the flailing Russian leader

insights.claim: Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov warned U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio at the end of May that Russia would launch 'systematic and sustained strikes' on facilities serving Ukraine's armed forces, including in Kyiv, and urged Western countries to evacuate diplomatic staff — an ultimatum the diplomats ignored.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A specific, dated escalation threat from Russia's foreign minister that was not acted upon by the West — a marker for tracking whether Kremlin rhetoric is followed by real action.
insights.quote: systematic and sustained strikes on facilities serving the needs of the Armed Forces of Ukraine in Kiev
insights.locator: At the end of May, Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov

insights.claim: After a Russian drone hit an apartment block in Romania, Deputy Security Council Chair Dmitry Medvedev warned Europeans that citizens of EU states should 'get used to' such incidents and 'will not be able to sleep peacefully.'
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that Russian strikes/spillover are already reaching NATO/EU territory (Romania), with Kremlin officials framing further such incidents as expected rather than accidental.
insights.quote: the citizens of [European Union] states, as the population of the belligerent countries, will not be able to sleep peacefully
insights.locator: Dmitry Medvedev... warned Europeans

insights.claim: Putin formally revised Russia's nuclear doctrine at the end of 2024 to significantly lower the threshold for nuclear weapon use, including declaring that aggression by a non-nuclear state against Russia could trigger nuclear retaliation if that state is backed by a nuclear power; Russia has also long hinted it has stationed nuclear weapons in Belarus.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A lowered, formally codified nuclear threshold is a structural (not just rhetorical) increase in tail risk that persists independent of any single incident.
insights.locator: the fact that Putin formally revised the country's official nuclear doctrine

insights.claim: In May, Russia and Belarus began joint military exercises that included preparations for nuclear weapons use, officially framed by Russia's Ministry of Defense as preempting alleged Western aggression.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Concrete, near-term operational evidence (not just doctrine) that nuclear-use rehearsal is part of current Russian military planning.
insights.locator: In May, the Russian Ministry of Defense announced

insights.claim: Former MI6 head Richard Moore said Putin is 'keen to expand the battlefield' by approving sabotage, cyberattacks, and arson in Britain in response to Russia's worsening military and economic position, part of a broader pattern of Russian cyberwarfare against European water systems, energy grids, and healthcare systems that has so far met limited direct Western response.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A former senior Western intelligence official explicitly warning of hybrid escalation against UK/European critical infrastructure — a direct risk signal for infrastructure and insurance exposure in Europe.
insights.locator: Former British MI6 head Richard Moore

insights.claim: Financial institutions in Russia were granted paramilitary powers in May, including the right to operate jamming devices and air defense weapons on their rooftops and for banking staff to carry guns to shoot down drones.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: An unusual, concrete indicator of how normalized drone-strike risk has become inside Russia — even civilian financial infrastructure is now arming against it.
insights.locator: In May, for example, Russian financial institutions

themes.theme: Why Putin can't easily end the war

insights.claim: Because military spending is now essentially the only thing powering Russian economic growth, cutting defense expenditure or agreeing to a settlement would itself cause a sharp economic contraction and hurt the procurement-fed wealth of Putin's cronies — creating a structural incentive to keep fighting even as the war becomes unaffordable.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific economic lock-in mechanism explaining why a negotiated Russian off-ramp is structurally harder than casualty/cost figures alone would suggest.
insights.locator: the transformation of the economy into one that builds and fuels a war machine

insights.claim: Putin has repeatedly rejected U.S. President Donald Trump's peace proposals even though those proposals were reportedly more favorable to Russia than Ukraine, because his actual goal remains subjugating all of Ukraine and denying it the ability to act as a sovereign state.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: Undercuts optimism that a favorable-to-Russia deal would end the war — Putin's stated goal is territorial/political subjugation, not a negotiated settlement on any terms.
insights.locator: He has repeatedly rejected peace proposals

insights.claim: Frankopan characterizes Putin, shaped by his formative KGB years, as a consistently poor strategist prone to paranoia and distrust who is likely to make decisions 'that make little sense for anyone outside the Kremlin' as his options narrow this summer.
insights.speaker: Peter Frankopan
insights.so_what: A psychological/decision-making framework for anticipating irrational or destabilizing Russian moves rather than assuming rational cost-benefit behavior.
insights.quote: decisions that make little sense for anyone outside the Kremlin
insights.locator: If a quarter of a century has taught one thing
